data.title: Hashkey has lost HK$3 billion in four years. What kind of business is it in?
data.description:
3月27日，HashKey 发布了 2025 年全年业绩，收入停滞、亏损持续、股价破发。

Hashkey4年累计亏损30亿。2022年亏损约3.82亿港元，2023年亏损约5.34亿港元，2024年亏损11.89亿港元，2025年亏损10.84亿港元。

2025年全年收入 7.23 亿港元，与去年的 7.21 亿几乎持平；净亏损 10.87 亿港元，较去年的 11.89 亿略有收窄。股价从上市高点 7.98 港元一路跌至最低 3.88 港元，近期虽回升至 4.72 港元附近，但距招股价 6.68 港元仍有相当差距。

要理解 HashKey 真正的商业逻辑，必须拆解财报深处的结构性变化。

散户大规模离场，机构资金持续流入

2025 年，HashKey 的总交易量达到 5908 亿港元，整体规模不降反升，但客户结构发生了根本性逆转。

机构客户交易量从去年的 2737 亿增至 4310 亿港元，综合账户（Omnibus）从 117 亿飙升至 864 亿港元。形成鲜明对比的是，散户交易量从 3530 亿骤降至 734 亿港元，降幅近 80%。

散户大规模离场、机构资金持续流入，直接导致了HashKey 面临典型的量增价跌困境，交易量的扩张并未能转化为收入的增长。

机构客户的费率远低于散户，高费率的散户收入跟着大幅流失；更麻烦的是，核心的交易促成板块，毛利率从 2024 年的 65.4% 暴跌到了2025 年的 41.0%，流动性准备金和数字资产持有成本把利润空间压得很薄。量在涨，利润率在塌，利润空间被两头挤。

稳定币占交易量48%，出入金才是核心需求

再往里看，有个数字很能说明问题：2025 年，稳定币交易量占到了集团数字资产总交易量的 48.0%，快一半了。同期，法币交易量从 1539 亿港元增至 2763 亿港元，涨了近 80%。

这两个数字合在一起意味着什么？来HashKey 的人，有近一半根本不是来炒币的，就是来换钱的——USDT 换港币，港币换 USDT，大额合规出入金。HashKey 实际上已经变成了一个 Web3 时代的外汇兑换通道，而不是大家印象里的加密货币交易所。

为什么会变成这样？几个原因叠在一起。

愿意进持牌平台的用户，本来就不是那种激进炒币的人，机构、家族办公室、传统金融客户进来，他们要的是大额资金安全合规地进进出出，不是加杠杆追涨杀跌，稳定币正好是这个需求的载体。

2025 年市场整体偏冷，资金倾向于退守稳定币或者换成法币等着，通道业务的占比自然被推高。

这也是 HashKey 自己选的方向。香港稳定币立法在稳步推进，因此，HashKey 明确把稳定币服务和 RWA 发行列为战略优先级。

成本结构持续高企，盈利模型仍未跑通

说到底，通道业务能不能赚钱？目前来看，很难。

核心问题不在单一业务，而在整体成本结构已经长期高于收入规模。2025 年公司总收入为 7.23 亿港元，但研发、人力与合规三项核心投入合计已明显超过这一水平。

其中，研发与技术投入约 5.04 亿港元，主要用于自建交易系统、HashKey Chain 及 RWA 基础设施；人力成本约 5.81 亿港元；全球多地牌照带来的合规成本也持续高企，仅上半年就超过 1.3 亿港元。

这些都是合规金融基础设施绕不开的结构性成本，短期很难压下来。

当然，财报里也不是没有好消息。资管业务 2025 年收入 1.17 亿港元，同比涨了 49.8%，毛利率高达 99.8%；链上服务毛利率也有 92.6%。对比之下，占总收入 72% 的交易促成业务毛利率只有 41%，差距很明显。

目前资管只占总收入 16%，但平台托管资产超过 184 亿港元，资管规模 72 亿港元，这块高毛利业务是将来能不能翻身的关键。

用持续的战略性亏损，换全牌照的合规地位

理解这家公司为什么敢这么烧，得先看看背后站着谁。

HashKey 的最大股东是万向集团掌门人鲁伟鼎，持股超过 40%；创始人肖风持股约 16%。肖风此前是博时基金创始人，业界称其"中国区块链教父"，是少数在传统金融和 Web3 两个世界都积累了深厚资源的人。

这个股东结构，是 HashKey 能在香港走通合规路线、拿到一张又一张牌照的核心底气——这不是一家普通创业公司在赌，背后有万向系的政商积累和肖风在传统金融的人脉网络在支撑。

拨开这些数字，HashKey 真正在卖的不是交易深度，是合规本身。

逻辑很清晰：用持续的战略性亏损，换全牌照的合规地位，换机构客户的市场份额，然后等香港数字资产市场成熟之后收割先发优势。

肖风的判断是，2026 年下半年是关键节点——Coinbase 和 Nasdaq 同步推出代币化股票，传统金融和链上金融真正融合的那一天，这条耗资数十亿建起来的合规通道，才会体现出真正的价值。

但这个逻辑能不能走通，取决于香港的 Web3 政策能不能持续兑现，全球竞争格局不发生大的偏移。在这一天到来之前，每年十亿的亏损缺口还要一直填下去，资本市场的耐心也会持续被消耗。

看 HashKey，看的不是它现在赚了多少，而是这条路能不能走通。这不是一个用财务模型定价的公司，押注的是香港数字资产监管能否真正落地，以及传统金融和 Web3 之间那扇门，最终由谁来开。

data.title: Trump: After Iran, Cuba is next.
data.description: "After Iran, next is Cuba." This statement by US President Trump on March 30th of this year has once again thrust an economy long relegated to the margins into the global spotlight. Understanding this statement merely as political rhetoric would underestimate its significance. More noteworthy is that even before similar statements emerged, the market had already begun to "price uncertainty" in advance. In mid-March, on a decentralized prediction platform, three accounts almost simultaneously established positions, betting that "the US will invade Cuba in 2026," totaling approximately $60,000. This behavior itself does not point to a definitive outcome, but it reflects a change: Cuba is re-entering the risk pricing system from a long-neglected variable. The background to this change is the continuously tightening real-world environment. In early 2026, the United States further strengthened its energy and trade restrictions on Cuba. On January 30, Trump signed an executive order declaring a national emergency and imposing tariffs on countries supplying oil to Cuba. The direct result was fuel shortages and widespread power outages in Cuba, putting pressure on both its economy and social environment. In this environment, the first thing to change is often not production, but a more fundamental issue: the smooth flow of capital. Cuba's cryptocurrency market gradually formed under this very question. In 2020, when Western Union closed its remittance channel from the United States to Cuba, a previously stable cross-border capital flow was severed. Many families reliant on overseas remittances were forced to seek alternative pathways. Against this backdrop, crypto assets, including Bitcoin, began to assume some of the function of cross-border value transfer. This stage was characterized by a clear pattern: demand preceded regulation, and usage preceded institutional framework. Subsequently, in 2021, the Central Bank of Cuba introduced a regulatory framework for virtual assets, implementing licensing management for virtual asset service providers and acknowledging their use within specific scopes. This did not mean that crypto assets were incorporated into the traditional financial system, but rather formed a model closer to "border management"—allowing their existence while emphasizing risk isolation. This institutional arrangement enabled the crypto market to move from a "spontaneous behavior" stage to an "observable and manageable" one. Around 2022, with the continuation of sanctions, Cuba began discussing alternative settlement pathways with countries like Russia, and crypto assets were included in the framework of cross-border payments discussions. At this point, its role had expanded from a "supplementary tool at the individual level" to a "potential settlement option." Looking at the overall picture from 2020 to 2026, a relatively clear evolutionary logic emerges: When traditional payment channels contracted, cryptocurrencies first appeared as an alternative; when this alternative was repeatedly used, it began to enter the regulatory purview; and when external constraints persisted, it was further incorporated into broader payment and settlement discussions. At the usage level, cryptocurrencies have been embedded in multiple scenarios in Cuba. On one hand, they are used for cross-border remittances and value transfers. Data shows that over 100,000 Cuban users are using Bitcoin and other crypto assets, with platforms like BitRemesas and QvaPay serving this demand. On the other hand, they are also beginning to enter more formal business environments. On March 23, 2026, the Central Bank of Cuba authorized ten companies for the first time to conduct cross-border commercial operations using virtual assets, allowing them to purchase, transfer, and hold assets within a licensed framework, and to disclose transaction details quarterly. This means that the role of crypto assets is extending from a "supplementary tool" to an "institutional tool." This evolution is not an isolated case when Cuba is placed within a larger framework. Sanctioned economies such as Iran are also exploring alternative payment paths, including cryptocurrencies. The difference lies in the fact that different countries develop different usage methods based on their own resources and constraints. Cuba's path focuses more on payment and circulation, rather than production or resource monetization. Returning to the initial question: Why did the market begin to act before Trump's statement? The fundamental reason lies in the fact that when uncertainty rises, the market not only reassesses the probability of the event itself, but also simultaneously evaluates a more fundamental variable: whether the flow of funds will change. In the Cuban context, cryptocurrency is part of these paths. From the disruption of remittances to the establishment of regulations, and then to its commercial use, Cuba's cryptocurrency market is not the result of a technological wave, but rather a path that gradually formed under real-world constraints. And as the external environment continues to change, this path itself is constantly being adjusted.

data.title: After Sora left the stage
data.description: A few days ago, OpenAI unexpectedly announced the shutdown of its AI video generation model, Sora. From its meteoric rise two years ago, touted as a game-changer for the film and television industry, to its quiet exit, Sora's short lifespan and abrupt turn of events are truly lamentable. In Silicon Valley, the closure of landmark projects often carries significant implications—like Apple's decisive abandonment of its car manufacturing plans after a decade-long journey, and Meta's drastic downsizing of its once-essential Metaverse Labs, the fall of these projects often accompanies strategic shifts within companies and even industries. Through OpenAI's choices and strategic moves, we can glimpse the changes taking place in the US AI industry. I interviewed several AI industry professionals, including researchers who have long traversed the US and Chinese AI circles and frontline practitioners. Some clues can be gleaned from the details they shared. "Resources" is a word repeatedly mentioned by industry insiders and is the starting point for understanding all these changes. A professional who has interacted with R&D personnel from leading US AI companies such as OpenAI and Meta told Tan Zhu that there is a general consensus in the industry that the competition in artificial intelligence ultimately boils down to a competition for resources. If talent, funding, and computing power are sufficient, AI companies usually invest in multiple technological directions simultaneously. OpenAI's shutdown of Sora is a result of resources becoming scarce. Let's look at the most fundamental issue: computing power. For the past two years, the US has been talking about building more data centers and continuing to expand computing power, but the reality is that supply has consistently failed to keep up with demand growth. According to Goldman Sachs' calculations, the supply-demand gap in US data center computing power is becoming the norm and is expected to remain at around 10 gigawatts for the next few years. More importantly, many new projects are stuck in the power and infrastructure环节, and will likely be difficult to implement in the end. This problem is even more pronounced for OpenAI. One characteristic of OpenAI is that it does not have its own data centers; its core computing power relies on cooperation with suppliers such as Microsoft. In 2025, the president of OpenAI mentioned that allocating GPUs internally had become a "very painful" process, requiring dedicated small teams to monitor when each project ended so that the freed-up computing power could be reallocated to new projects. It was precisely at this time that a second pressure began to emerge: the AI ​​industry was entering a new capital cycle. Compared to the past "invest first, then validate" approach, investors began to strongly demand measurable commercial returns. A survey by a US consulting firm shows that 53% of investors expect a return within six months. More than 70% of CIOs have explicitly stated that if they cannot prove the commercial value of AI by mid-2026, related budgets will be cut. When capital requirements increase and computing power becomes expensive and scarce, companies are forced to make choices. This change is particularly dramatic for current US AI companies. It is estimated that Sora costs $1.30 to generate a 10-second video. Assuming users generate 11.3 million videos per day, the daily computing cost is approximately $15 million, while actual revenue is far lower. Sora is just one example of this "trade-off logic": || In the past month, ChatGPT's native quick payment function has also been adjusted. || At the same time, OpenAI announced an agreement with the US Department of Defense to deploy OpenAI's models on a classified military network. || These canceled projects share common characteristics: consumer-grade positioning, extremely high computing power consumption, stagnant user growth, and unclear short-term returns. || Behind this is a structural adjustment undergoing in the US AI industry: || Meta announced in March 2026 that it plans to shut down Horizon Worlds support for VR headsets on June 15th. Most of its budget will be invested in AI infrastructure, wearable devices, and mobile platforms. Amazon announced the closure of its Amazon Fresh and Amazon Go physical store pilot programs, and the discontinuation of its Amazon One palmprint payment system. Scale AI, a leading company in AI data labeling, restructured its team in July 2025, increasing resources for enterprise and government sales and focusing its data labeling business on the most profitable opportunities. A consensus is emerging in the US AI industry to prioritize high-margin projects and reduce continued investment in consumer-level innovation. So, what kind of projects are considered "high-margin"? Recently, OpenAI reached an agreement with the US Department of Defense to deploy advanced models on a classified military network. The unique aspect of this choice lies in crossing two red lines. One is OpenAI's own red line. OpenAI, which had repeatedly emphasized that it would not provide technical support for large-scale domestic surveillance or autonomous weapons systems, has now changed its stance. Secondly, it represents the industry's bottom line. Another leading US AI company, Anthropic, was listed as a "supply chain risk" by the Pentagon for adhering to this bottom line, and OpenAI took over at the same time. This reveals the dangerous trend in the next stage of US AI development: areas previously avoided are now being touched; bottom lines previously upheld are now being breached. Chen Qi, who participated in 14 rounds of Sino-US AI dialogues, told Tan Zhu that the mainstream of the US strategic community and businesses is strongly advocating for an AI "arms race," pursuing absolute superiority over other countries. Especially in the past year, the current US government has completely overturned the previous government's concept of "safe, reliable, and trustworthy AI," shifting to "winning the AI ​​race," placing defeating other countries at the absolute core. This paves the way for a deep integration of capital and the military. Under pressure to "win the competition," previously established boundaries are being redefined, making the Department of Defense a highly attractive partner. Vertically, in the three major areas of serving consumers (To C), businesses (To B), and the public sector (To G), the public sector is becoming the last "blue ocean" for AI. In terms of deployment speed, U.S. public sector professionals use artificial intelligence far less frequently than most Americans. This signifies a vast untapped potential. It also means that the public sector has not yet entered the "return-on-performance" stage. Here, budgets come from appropriations rather than profit requirements, resulting in longer cycles, more stable funding, and a higher risk tolerance. Horizontally, within the public sector, the Department of Defense has the highest willingness to cooperate. According to statistics on U.S. federal government contracts, the Department of Defense accounts for over 70% of federal government AI contracts. From September 2022 to August 2023 alone, the value of Department of Defense AI contracts increased by 1500%. Liu Wei, who has exchanged views with delegations from U.S. think tanks and the military, told Tan Zhu that, based on his observations, companies choose to cooperate with the military partly due to practical business pressures, and partly because they want to achieve technological breakthroughs through the military as a "testing ground." This cooperation is a key element in the US's construction of AI hegemony—it deeply binds technological research and development with military capabilities, uses public funds to pave the way for capital, and then uses technological advantages to further fuel military hegemony. However, the costs are now emerging. Originally, the US government's slow deployment of AI had its own inherent logic. Unlike the private sector, certain shortcomings of AI in the public sector—such as AI illusions and insufficient accuracy—may be merely user experience issues in the private sector, but could escalate into social problems: || Statistics departments: AI-assisted data processing can significantly improve efficiency, but AI illusions leading to numerical errors could directly impact the economy and people's livelihoods; || Agriculture, energy, and other sectors: AI can assist in scientific research and resource allocation, but AI illusions may lead to erroneous model predictions; || Defense sector: Previously, some AI projects experienced target recognition systems with an accuracy rate of only about 30% in severe weather; errors in decisions involving human lives can have irreversible consequences. Now, this caution is being abandoned. The Department of Defense has adopted a radical "use and modify" approach—rapidly deploying AI into real-world combat scenarios and iterating and optimizing based on actual feedback. This approach itself risks human lives for the speed of technological iteration. Liu Wei told Tan Zhu that, especially among the mid-to-low-level U.S. military officers he's spoken with—those actually fighting on the front lines—they are quite enthusiastic about the militarized application of artificial intelligence, generally believing it helps improve combat capabilities and strategic planning. This reveals the dangerous trend of U.S. AI hegemony reflected in OpenAI's choice: Capital seeks returns, the U.S. government seeks military advantage, and both sides compromise and leverage each other in the field of AI, jointly crossing ethical and security boundaries. The U.S. has chosen to prioritize AI deployment in the military field. Where will the next boundary be pushed to?

data.title: FBI Director's Porn Account  Leak Sparks Memecoin Scam Frenzy on Solana’s Pump.fun
data.description:
Hackers expose “Spiderkash” — and crypto pounces

A viral data leak tied to the director of the FBI-Kash Patel-has ignited a wave of memecoin scams on Pump.fun, with opportunistic traders launching dozens of tokens that surged and crashed by over 90% within hours.

The chaos began after an Iranian-linked hacking collective leaked more than 300 emails, personal photos, and a resume allegedly tied to Patel. Among the most viral details was a burner alias — “spiderkash” — linked to a Yahoo email address that quickly spread across social media.

$Spiderkash - EJ6WpzXDsykFBECHKtGJC7pm34TZLVn4FBn3i3bPzyXY

Kash Patel’s porn site account has been discovered from a username leak and it's going super viral on X.

This post alone from @RightWingCope has gained almost 10M views in just a few hours, absolutely MAD VIRAL.… pic.twitter.com/rL8DauaDWD

— Kimchi ✴️ (@Kimchi_Dev) March 28, 2026

Online investigators soon connected the handle to a profile on the adult platform XVideos. While there is no confirmation the account belongs to Patel, the exact username match was enough to ignite speculation and drive millions of views across X within hours.

The Only leaked Video from the Kash Patel's Gmail account, Dancing with an Indian song pic.twitter.com/VqGUPtwadH

— HP_ClipStorm (@HINDUSTAN_PLUSE) March 29, 2026

With no official response from Patel, the controversy created the perfect vacuum for crypto opportunists to move in.

Pump.fun turns scandal into instant pump-and-dump tokens

Almost immediately, promoters began flooding Solana with “Spiderkash”-themed memecoins via Pump.fun, a platform known for its frictionless token launches and viral speculation cycles.

One token named directly after the leaked email address skyrocketed within a minute of launch before collapsing 87% just five minutes later, never recovering. Another briefly reached a $104,000 market cap before shedding over 70% in under an hour and more than 90% by the next day.

14M+ views on one post and others doing millions as well.
this is not normal attention.
whole timeline is flooded.
when something hits this level of virality, it usually sends way higher.$Spiderkash feels like it’s just getting started. https://t.co/MWoRppZkLOpic.twitter.com/YS0E2dT6OF

— Professional Rug Victim (@rugged_butalive) March 28, 2026

Even smaller tokens followed the same pattern. A coin dubbed “Mayhem” spiked briefly before crashing more than 80% in seconds, while a single wallet deployed multiple Spiderkash tokens simultaneously — none sustained meaningful trading activity beyond short-lived price spikes.

Across the board, nearly all tokens tied to the controversy were trading more than 90% below their peaks within hours, reinforcing how fast-moving speculation on Pump.fun often ends in losses for late entrants.

From geopolitical hack to memecoin exploitation

The incident highlights how quickly real-world events can be turned into financial speculation in crypto markets. What began as a politically charged data leak rapidly transformed into a trading frenzy, where attention — not truth — became the primary driver of value.

The hackers behind the leak had previously targeted Patel in 2024, framing their actions as retaliation against U.S. enforcement actions. But in the memecoin economy, the motive mattered far less than the momentum.

📉REKT: 96% OF PUMP. FUN TRADERS LOST MONEY THIS MONTH

Per @TedPillows, 96% of users who traded @PumpFun tokens this month incurred losses.

The remaining 4% of the profit, he argues, is likely held by insiders and team members with information advantages unavailable to retail… pic.twitter.com/3oXVEvnrKB

— BSCN (@BSCNews) March 24, 2026

In the end, the “Spiderkash” saga underscores a growing reality in crypto: any viral moment — whether verified or not — can be instantly monetized, with memecoin creators racing to capture attention before it fades. For most traders, however, the outcome remains the same — fleeting hype followed by steep losses.

data.title: HashKey's annual report reveals a loss of 1 billion yuan, with stablecoin trading volume accounting for nearly half.
data.description:
Tao Zhu, Jinse Finance

On March 27, 2026, HashKey released its first annual results announcement since its listing, disclosing its operating and financial performance for the year ended December 31, 2025.

In 2025, the company's revenue was basically flat, but its profit still suffered a significant loss, and its gross profit margin declined significantly. However, from the perspective of business structure, its institutional transformation and compliance advantages are becoming new growth engines.

I. HashKey's Overall Operating and Financial Situation in 2025

HashKey's revenue remained relatively stable during the year, at HK$723.1 million (Fiscal Year 2024: HK$720.7 million).

The net loss for the year was HK$1,084.3 million (Fiscal Year 2024: HK$1,189.6 million).

The adjusted annual loss (non-IFRS measured) for the year was HK$736.6 million (FY2024: HK$545.2 million). The debt-to-equity ratio (total liabilities divided by total assets, expressed as a percentage) for 2025 was 31.9% (December 31, 2024: 165.2%). Revenue for the year ended December 31, 2025 remained relatively stable at HK$723.1 million. The table below sets out revenue by revenue source for the years ended December 31, 2025 and 2024.

Of HashKey's annual revenue, transaction facilitation services still account for the largest share at 72.3%; on-chain services account for 11.5%; and asset management services account for 16.2%.

It is worth noting that within "transaction facilitation services," the sluggish crypto market led to a direct decrease in retail trading volume—from HK$638.4 billion in fiscal year 2024 to HK$590.8 billion.

HashKey strategically focused on its Hong Kong platform and optimized its operations. Through expanded institutional partnerships, compliant fiat currency deposits and withdrawals, and a wider product offering, it enhanced monetization capabilities and trading activity, driving platform asset growth and attracting institutional investor participation, thereby facilitating large-scale transactions. As a result, trading volume from Hong Kong increased significantly from HK$307.6 billion in fiscal year 2024 to HK$530 billion. Furthermore, revenue from on-chain services decreased by 33.3% year-on-year to HK$83.2 million, compared to HK$124.8 million in fiscal year 2024. The decline was primarily due to price fluctuations of tokens related to the proof-of-stake consensus mechanism during a sluggish market.

II. HashKey's Future Business Layout

1. Stablecoin Business

Against the backdrop of increased volatility and declining risk appetite in the current crypto market, stablecoins are gradually evolving from simple mediums of exchange into the underlying financial infrastructure of the entire digital asset system.

HashKey stated in its financial report: Stablecoin-related services are a key strategic focus of the Group, facilitating the effective exchange between fiat currency and digital assets through its trading platform.

HashKey stated in its financial report: Stablecoin-related services are a key strategic focus of the Group, facilitating the effective exchange between fiat currency and digital assets through its trading platform.

This strategic focus benefited from a significant increase in fiat currency trading volume from HK$153.9 billion in FY2024 to HK$276.3 billion, primarily due to market participants shifting their preference towards fiat currency channels amid a market downturn. HashKey is actively strengthening its fiat currency deposit and withdrawal channels across multiple jurisdictions. Stablecoins have become an integral part of the Group's ecosystem, accounting for 48.0% of its digital asset trading volume in FY2025. HashKey supports trading and settlement of primarily regulated stablecoins and provides services to licensed stablecoin participants in Hong Kong, thereby improving liquidity, settlement efficiency, and institutional adoption. HashKey's emphasis on strengthening the "fiat currency deposit/withdrawal + stablecoin" combination essentially aims to build a closed-loop channel for mutual conversion between traditional finance and digital assets. The 48.0% trading volume share also reflects the annual increase in HashKey's compliant OTC trading. In a context of increasingly stringent regulations, developing compliant stablecoins is paramount; stablecoins are not merely products and payment methods, but also a reflection of compliance capabilities. Furthermore, compliant custody, efficient clearing, and low volatility are core requirements for institutional investors entering the crypto market, and stablecoins meet these needs. Therefore, the increased proportion of stablecoins is essentially a signal of increased institutionalization. In the stablecoin business, HashKey has a natural advantage in the regulatory field. HashKey supports regulated stablecoin trading and settlement, serves Hong Kong licensed stablecoin participants, and is deeply integrated with the fiat currency clearing system, which will further gain higher institutional trust and break down cooperation barriers. Moreover, with increasingly stringent regulations in the crypto industry, HashKey's advantages may become even more prominent. 2. RWA Business Stablecoins solve the problem of fund inflows and outflows, while RWA solves the problem of what needs to be on-chain. In the current process of the crypto industry's transformation from transaction-driven to asset-driven, RWA is gradually becoming one of the most certain long-term directions. HashKey stated in its financial report: In terms of on-chain services, the Group will continue to develop the HashKey chain for institutional applications, promote the tokenization, staking, and on-chain settlement of real-world assets, and encourage financial institutions and enterprises to adopt its crypto-as-a-service solutions. In its real-world asset tokenization business, HashKey will continue to develop a one-stop solution for real-world asset tokenization. Based on the latest regulatory guidelines issued in mainland China, both equity-based and asset-backed real-world asset tokenization businesses have established clear regulatory paths, highlighting the enormous market potential for institutions participating in real-world asset tokenization under a compliant framework. HashKey's financial report clearly emphasizes its strengthened RWA (Real-World Asset Tokenization) strategy, which has already made substantial progress: completing multiple collaborative projects covering financial institutions, real estate, and cross-border assets, deploying 11 tokenized products on-chain, achieving a total real-world asset value of approximately HK$2 billion, and launching the world's first tokenized money market ETF. This means that HashKey is not merely at the conceptual level but has entered the productization and commercialization stage. 3. Integrating AI Currently, AI is gradually evolving from a single-point tool into an institutional-level core capability and is deeply embedded in financial infrastructure. In response to this trend, HashKey has elevated AI to a strategic level, aiming to build the next generation of digital financial infrastructure through the integration of "AI + Web3". HashKey stated in its financial report: Currently, AI is evolving from a personal productivity tool to an institutional-level core capability, becoming a fundamental part of our future product ecosystem. Web3 and AI have become major drivers of industry innovation. The decentralized nature of digital assets and the Web3 ecosystem naturally aligns with the requirements of AI agents, jointly catalyzing a new growth cycle in this field. HashKey is integrating AI into all workflows to drive efficiency improvements and cost optimization. Currently, AI is embedded in risk management and security frameworks. Looking ahead, HashKey will move towards an AI-driven R&D lifecycle and introduce autonomous agent processing into the daily context of enterprise functions. Strategically, HashKey will prioritize AI-powered agent payment infrastructure as the intersection of AI and Web3. In the AI ​​agent ecosystem, digital assets possess significant advantages: as a native value carrier with decentralized trust mechanisms and on-chain programmable capabilities, they are particularly suitable for meeting the value exchange requirements of AI agents as independent digital entities. For HashKey, AI is no longer just an optimization tool, but a fundamental capability permeating trading, risk control, operations, and R&D. The crypto field has a natural affinity for AI. First, crypto assets suffer from high volatility, high leverage, and high technical risks, while AI can automatically identify risks, monitor abnormal transactions, and perform anti-money laundering and fund tracking. Second, AI can significantly improve the platform's trading efficiency, such as optimizing order matching and liquidity allocation. Finally, given HashKey's continued losses, AI can significantly reduce costs and is expected to optimize workflows, helping HashKey further reduce costs and increase efficiency. III. Conclusion On December 17th last year, HashKey went public, becoming one of the first licensed crypto asset platforms to list in Asia. However, this pioneer who ushered in the era of compliant crypto exchanges has consistently suffered losses, and its financial reports remain unfavorable. The financial reports show that HashKey has tried almost every crypto business, yet profitability remains limited. In trading, HashKey offers spot trading, OTC, institutional accounts, fiat currency, stablecoins, and token listing services; on-chain services include staking, public chains, RWA, tokenized ETFs, and CaaS; in asset management, it offers digital asset funds, primary and secondary market investments, and asset management services; and it also offers custody, compliance, security, AI+Web3, and platform token services. It can be said that HashKey is in a typical investment phase: revenue growth has stagnated, profits are continuously incurring losses, gross margins are under pressure, and its financial performance has not yet shown a turning point.

data.title: BTC miners are collectively turning to Bitcoin.
data.description: Many people's impression of Bitcoin miners is still limited to buying mining rigs, mining BTC, and waiting for the price to rise. In reality, an increasing number of large BTC miners are actively aligning themselves with AI infrastructure companies. They are selling their Bitcoin holdings while investing the funds in AI data centers, GPU clouds, HPC facilities, and power parks. This reflects a shift in the entire business and valuation logic behind BTC mining companies. MARA sold 15,133 BTC, cashing out approximately $1.1 billion. This money was primarily used to repurchase debt, optimize its balance sheet, and make room for expansion in AI and digital infrastructure. Cipher Digital is pushing forward with long-term data center park leases, increasingly resembling an infrastructure operator serving hyperscalers. IREN is directly betting on AI Cloud, focusing its future growth on expanding GPU and cloud computing capabilities. Hut 8, Core Scientific, and TeraWulf are also shifting their resources—electricity, land, data centers, and grid connections—originally used for mining, to AI computing power. The core logic behind this is: First, after the halving cycle, the profit margin for pure mining has been significantly compressed. Decreasing revenue and intensified competition mean more and more mining companies are facing greater pressure to break even. Second, the truly valuable assets held by mining companies include low-cost electricity, land, power substations, cooling systems, and data center maintenance capabilities. These are precisely the scarcest underlying resources for AI data centers. Third, the capital market is giving AI infrastructure a higher valuation. The market's pricing methods for the same company differ significantly between mining companies and data center platforms. Previously, the market preferred the narrative of miners accumulating Bitcoin, as this represented a bet on Bitcoin's long-term rise. Now, these companies are choosing to sell their BTC and invest in AI, indicating that management sees the certainty of cash flow and growth potential from AI infrastructure as more attractive. Future leading mining companies are likely to gradually evolve into a type of hybrid company—connecting the crypto industry upstream and energy, electricity, and data center infrastructure downstream, transforming from companies reliant on cryptocurrency price fluctuations into platforms with electricity and computing power as their core assets.

data.title: “Mined in America Act” Targets China’s Grip on Bitcoin Mining, Pushes U.S. Toward Full Industry Independence
data.description:
Senators Introduces Bill To Restrict Bitcoin Mining Within Home Boundaries

U.S. Senators Bill Cassidy and Cynthia Lummis have unveiled the “Mined in America Act,” a sweeping proposal aimed at breaking America’s dependence on foreign Bitcoin mining hardware while accelerating the creation of a national Strategic Bitcoin Reserve.

🚨 SENATOR CYNTHIA LUMMIS HAS INTRODUCED THE “MINED IN AMERICA ACT” 🇺🇸

THE BILL WILL BOOST DOMESTIC $BTC MINING AND PHASE OUT FOREIGN ADVERSARY HARDWARE

IT ALSO SEEKS TO CODIFY A U.S. STRATEGIC BITCOIN RESERVE WITHIN THE TREASURY

BULLISH FOR BITCOIN 🚀 pic.twitter.com/pztIcdHI98

— BlockNews (@blocknewsdotcom) March 30, 2026

At the heart of the bill is a voluntary “Mined in America” certification program designed to reshape the mining industry’s supply chain. Facilities that opt in would be required to phase out equipment tied to foreign adversaries — particularly Chinese manufacturers like Bitmain and MicroBT — and transition toward domestically produced hardware.

In return, certified miners would gain access to federal support, including energy and rural development programs, effectively creating a government-backed pathway for U.S.-based mining expansion.

The bill also directs agencies like the National Institute of Standards and Technology to help accelerate the development of secure, energy-efficient American mining rigs, mirroring past efforts to localize semiconductor production.

For lawmakers, the message is clear: Bitcoin mining is no longer just a private industry — it is becoming a matter of economic sovereignty. As Cassidy put it

“Digital asset mining is a big part of our economy. We should be doing it here in America.This bill will secure supply chains, back U.S manufacturing and support this industy.”

Supply chain risks expose China’s dominance

The urgency behind the bill stems from a stark reality: while the U.S. accounts for roughly 38% of global Bitcoin hashrate, it remains heavily reliant on foreign hardware.

NEW: According to analysis from Hashrate Index, rising oil prices have little direct impact on Bitcoin mining costs since most of the network runs on energy sources that are largely independent of crude oil; only about 6–10% of global hashrate operates in oil-sensitive power… pic.twitter.com/yGJQeUzVmH

— Bitcoin News (@BitcoinNewsCom) March 14, 2026

Chinese firms, particularly Bitmain and MicroBT, continue to dominate the mining equipment market — with some estimates suggesting they underpin the vast majority of global infrastructure.

Recent disruptions have exposed just how fragile that dependence is. U.S. miners faced shipment delays in March after authorities halted imported rigs over technical concerns.

Policymakers including Elizabeth Warren have additionally raised alarms about potential national security risks due to the overreliance on foreign-built machines. These concerns range from supply chain choke points to fears of embedded vulnerabilities in critical infrastructure.

Bloomberg reports that Bitmain, the manufacturer of Bitcoin-mining machines, has been under a U.S. national security investigation over concerns its hardware could be remotely manipulated or used for espionage. The probe was triggered by Bitmain devices operating near a U.S.…

— Wu Blockchain (@WuBlockchain) November 21, 2025

Against this backdrop, the bill reframes mining hardware as a strategic asset. Lawmakers and industry advocates argue that relying on overseas manufacturers — especially those linked to geopolitical rivals — creates unacceptable risks for a network that underpins billions in value.

Strategic Bitcoin Reserve and national ambition

Beyond hardware independence, the legislation also reinforces efforts to formalize a Strategic Bitcoin Reserve, aligning with broader ambitions championed by Donald Trump to position the U.S. as the global hub for digital assets.

💥 Jim Cramer says President Trump purchased Bitcoin for the US strategic reserve during the crash this week.

"I heard at $60k he's gonna fill the Bitcoin Reserve." pic.twitter.com/9z4U8FxaRo

— BITCOIN EXPERT INDIA (@Btcexpertindia) February 8, 2026

The reserve concept would treat Bitcoin as a strategic national holding, similar to gold or energy reserves, while anchoring long-term government involvement in the crypto economy.

Combined with the certification framework, the bill effectively links domestic production, national security, and financial strategy into a single policy direction.

Supporters also point to economic benefits, arguing that expanding domestic mining and manufacturing could drive job creation, particularly as data center demand surges alongside the AI boom.

The “Mined in America Act” signals a broader shift: Bitcoin mining is no longer just about cheap power and hash rate — it is becoming a geopolitical battleground. By pushing for onshore production and reducing reliance on foreign technology, U.S. lawmakers are attempting to reclaim control over the infrastructure behind the world’s largest cryptocurrency network.

🇺🇸🔥 TRUMP: THE BITCOIN PRESIDENT IS HERE!

• Crypto future → Made in the USA⁰• US to become the Crypto Capital of the World⁰• Bitcoin mining shifting to America⁰• Strategic Bitcoin Reserve (SBR) coming⁰• $BTC treated as a national reserve asset

Biden-era crackdown ❌… pic.twitter.com/VUcgOQHskl

— SMC KAPIL DEV (@smckapildev) January 17, 2026

If successful, the bill could reshape global mining dynamics, weakening China’s long-standing hardware dominance while cementing the U.S. as both the largest mining hub and a self-sufficient one. But the transition will not be easy, as the industry must rebuild supply chains that have been entrenched overseas for years.

What is clear, however, is the direction of travel: the era of globally dependent Bitcoin mining is being challenged — and Washington wants the next phase of the industry to be built at home.

data.title: US Department of Labor Proposes Rule Allowing 401(k) Plans to Include Crypto and Alternative Investments for Retirement Savers: What Are the Risks?
data.description:
US Department of Labor Proposes Rule To Let 401(k) Plans Include Crypto And Alternative Assets

Americans could soon have the option to invest their 401(k) retirement savings in cryptocurrencies, private equity, and other alternative assets, under a proposed rule released by the US Department of Labor.

The draft rule offers fiduciaries a clear process to follow, giving them a safe harbour when considering more complex investments for retirement plans.

JUST IN: 🇺🇸 The US Department of Labor has proposed a rule that would allow Bitcoin investments in 401(k) plans. pic.twitter.com/P8CcPDzDEP

— Crypto Briefing (@Crypto_Briefing) March 30, 2026

How Will Safe Harbour Work For 401(k) Managers

The proposal allows plan fiduciaries to access legal protection if they conduct thorough reviews on factors including performance, fees, liquidity, valuation, benchmarking, and complexity before offering alternative investments.

Once they document this process, managers can include options such as crypto or private equity without fear of legal challenges.

Joshua Chu, lawyer and co-chair of the Hong Kong Web3 Association, said the rule would give fiduciaries a “clear safe harbour roadmap” and allow retirement savers to access alternative assets more confidently, without plan sponsors fearing regulatory backlash.

The proposal follows the Department’s decision last May to rescind Biden-era guidance that had urged extreme caution before adding crypto to 401(k) menus, which the agency said went beyond federal law requirements.

Trump Administration Push For Expanded 401(k) Choices

The draft rule implements an executive order from President Donald Trump in August 2025 aimed at broadening 401(k) investment options.

US Secretary of Labor Lori Chavez-DeRemer said,

“This greater diversity will drive innovation and result in a major win for American workers, retirees, and their families.”

Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent added that the measure seeks to expand choices in a “safe and smart manner.”

The proposal would affect roughly 721,000 retirement plans covering around 118 million workers and $8.8 trillion in assets, based on Labor Department estimates.

U.S. PROPOSES OPENING 401(k)s TO CRYPTO -- AND THE SCALE IS MASSIVE 👀

The Labor Department just proposed rules to allow crypto and alternative assets into retirement plans covering ~118M Americans and ~$8.8T in assets.

This doesn’t auto-add crypto, but it gives fiduciaries a… pic.twitter.com/Gs8VxrGaZI

— CryptosRus (@CryptosR_Us) March 31, 2026

Overall, Americans held approximately $10.1 trillion in 401(k) accounts at the end of 2025, within a $14.2 trillion defined contribution market, according to the Investment Company Institute.

Currently, only 4% of these plans offer alternative investments, with just 0.1% of assets allocated to them.

Risks And Opportunities Of Alternative Assets In Retirement Plans

Supporters argue alternative funds, including crypto, can improve returns over the long term and provide diversification beyond stocks and bonds.

Andrew M. Bailey, Senior Fellow at the Bitcoin Policy Institute, said,

“Retirement funds are the holy grail for bitcoin enthusiasts looking for new investors: oceans of cash, tax-advantaged.”

However, the long-term horizon of retirement plans often conflicts with the inherent risk of these assets.

Bailey noted,

“Their approach to risk and tight regulations pulls them in the opposite direction… rule changes that empower savers to make their own choices would be welcome.”

Wall Street firms have begun signalling potential allocations for crypto within portfolios.

Morgan Stanley has advised a 2% to 4% allocation for investors, while BlackRock recommends a more modest 1% to 2% exposure for diversified portfolios.

Source: X

Could 401(k) Crypto Access Change The Market

Expanding access to digital assets in retirement accounts may also influence demand for other investment products, such as equity-based bitcoin vehicles.

Bailey remarked that whether direct 401(k) exposure would cannibalise or complement these offerings remains an open question.

Fiduciaries will still need to implement controls for daily pricing, liquidity, and risk management before crypto reaches participants’ accounts.

Joshua Chu noted that US retirees could gain earlier access to regulated crypto exposure than most Asian savers, pointing to Hong Kong’s pension system and China’s trading ban as ongoing barriers in the region.

Concerns Over Investor Safety And Market Timing

Some critics have expressed concern about exposing retirement savings to higher-risk assets.

Senator Elizabeth Warren (D-Massachusetts) said,

“Americans facing an uncertain future in Trump’s economy will now have more reasons to question the security of their retirement savings — all so that Trump’s Wall Street buddies have another pile of cash to play with.”

The proposal comes at a sensitive time.

While crypto markets have shown relative resilience, the private credit sector has faced pressure, with major firms including BlackRock and Apollo limiting investor withdrawals amid growing redemption requests.

Gradual Adoption Expected As Managers Adjust

Legal experts suggest the rule’s impact may unfold gradually as fiduciaries become comfortable that their investment choices will not trigger lawsuits.

Erin K. Cho of Mayer Brown said,

“By gradually reducing the amount of frivolous litigation over time, the hope is that the regulation will enable plan fiduciaries to consider a wider range of asset classes and investment alternatives for their plan participants.”

The rule is now open for public feedback before it can be formally adopted, marking a potential shift in how Americans can save for retirement while participating in newer, alternative markets.

data.title: Cryptocurrency CEXs are becoming a thing of the past.
data.description:
Author: momo, ChainCatcher

Bitcoin is underperforming gold, silver, crude oil, and tech stocks; the altcoin season has almost disappeared, and the voices saying "crypto has entered garbage time" are growing louder. But it is precisely in this so-called garbage time that Crypto natives are forced to understand the world, and a profound reconstruction of the future of trading is taking place.

I. Crypto Natives Forced to Understand the World

If we look at two sets of data together, we may have some different views on the argument that "crypto has entered garbage time."

One ​​set is the emerging TradFi trading boom in crypto. In the past year, whether it is gold, US stocks, or commodities such as crude oil, they have been constantly absorbing global liquidity; and within crypto trading platforms, the trading volume of TradFi assets is also increasing simultaneously.

Recently, RWA trading volume on Hyperliquid has continued to reach new highs; Binance's gold and silver contract trading volumes have also hit record highs; and Bitget's CFD section, which includes 79 popular trading instruments such as gold, silver, and crude oil, recently saw its daily trading volume exceed $6 billion, setting a new record. To put this into perspective, Binance's recent daily spot trading volume is only around $8 billion. This also means that in a bear market, for crypto traders, "leaving" is no longer the only option as before. Instead, they can remain within their crypto accounts, unrestricted by location or market closures, and directly switch to TradFi assets to seek new profit opportunities or hedge risks. Despite the constant complaints from crypto enthusiasts about being stuck in "garbage time," this phase has forced them to learn about and understand the world, paying attention to variables they previously overlooked: the Federal Reserve's interest rate path, inflation data, the AI ​​industry cycle, and even the supply and demand structure of crude oil. This shift has even spilled over into professional content production. Whether in the media or among KOLs, the topics discussed have clearly expanded—macroeconomics, AI, and commodities are appearing alongside crypto, no longer just background information. A KOL recently compiled statistics showing that half of the content in many crypto media outlets is no longer "purely crypto," but rather includes a large amount of AI and traditional asset content. Even crypto CEXs like Bitget are gradually transforming their daily market reports into a mixed stream of information on macroeconomics, TradeFi, AI, and crypto. Another set of data reveals an even more counterintuitive shift in user flow. In the past, bull markets attracted users through the wealth effect, while bear markets often saw a user exodus. However, according to a report by @smartestxyz, there's a metric called "Non-Crypto-First Users"—users whose first on-chain transaction was RWA Perp, not Crypto. As of March 2026, there were nearly 50,000 such users, whose first encounter with Crypto wasn't because of Bitcoin, but because of stock indices, gold, or crude oil. This means that bear markets can still attract new users, and the motivations of these newcomers have changed. They are not attracted by the "get rich overnight" crypto narrative, but rather by the convenience of on-chain finance, which addresses the pain points of high barriers to entry and low efficiency in traditional finance. In other words, Crypto is no longer relying solely on narratives and airdrops to attract new users, but is starting to acquire customers by "solving real transaction needs." The value of Crypto presented by these two sets of data contradicts the notion of "garbage time" to some extent. Perhaps more accurately, the current crypto market is superficially quiet, but internally undergoing restructuring. If the past Crypto market was more like a narrative-driven market, it is now entering a phase driven by real demand. In a sense, this may be the beginning of its true growth. II. "Crypto CEXs" May Become a Historical Species However, the migration of users within and outside the crypto space to TradFi may cause "Crypto CEXs" to exit the historical stage. This doesn't mean that Crypto CEXs will disappear immediately, but rather that exchanges that only deal with crypto assets may not last long in the future. For crypto centralized exchanges (CEXs), this crisis was already evident in the 2024 bull market. Crypto CEXs failed to see the anticipated large-scale influx of users from outside the crypto space, and the decline of traffic dividends has become an industry consensus. Crypto CEXs' reliance on subsidies and trading rebates to generate trading volume is becoming inefficient and unsustainable. The underlying reason is simple: besides crypto assets, the demand for multi-asset trading in TradFi and on-chain assets is no longer a short-term need, but rather a new normal for smoothing cyclical fluctuations. For a long time, the crypto market was a relatively independent and self-consistent system; narratives, liquidity, and price cycles primarily occurred within the crypto sphere. However, in the last 1-2 years, this "self-consistency" has been broken. The simple bull-bear cycle model of the past four years is no longer effective. Surviving a bear market doesn't guarantee a broad-based bull market, and airdrops have lost their effectiveness. Bitcoin is increasingly embedded in macroeconomic cycles; it's no longer just a "crypto asset" but has become part of global liquidity. In this context, Crypto investors are naturally no longer satisfied with a single crypto position but aspire to leverage the liquidity of crypto assets to capture the alpha and cyclicality of mainstream global assets. The explosive growth of the RWA market illustrates this point. Recent data from RWA.xyz shows that, excluding stablecoins, the total value of on-chain tokenized real-world assets has exceeded $25 billion, nearly four times the $6.4 billion a year ago. Currently, six asset classes have on-chain assets exceeding $1 billion, including US Treasury bonds, commodities, private credit, institutional alternative investment funds, corporate bonds, and non-US government debt.

III. UEX Takes the Lead, a Covert War Has Begun

If the "crypto CEX" model gradually fades from the historical stage, what will the next generation of trading apps be? Mainstream crypto exchanges and TradFi institutions are waging a covert war around this theme.

1. Restructuring the Trading System of Crypto CEXs

Many people have noticed that mainstream exchanges such as Binance, OKX, Bitget, and Bybit are listing TradFi assets. However, most people understand this as another round of "hot topic narratives" similar to Chinese memes or AI.

But one detail is often overlooked: some exchanges, represented by Bitget, no longer place TradFi in the second or third level menu, but directly place it in the first level entry alongside Crypto.

This is somewhat similar to how Alibaba and JD.com, in their food delivery wars, placed food delivery entry points directly in the core positions of their main websites. This isn't just about "adding a new category," but a shift in platform focus. In other words, TradeFi is different from past memes and AI initiatives. It's not simply a new asset listing, but more like an adjustment to the trading structure and strategic direction. Against this backdrop, the concept of UEX (Universal Exchange) becomes easier to understand. Originally proposed by Bitget, this concept essentially aims to allow users to complete multi-asset transactions within a single platform through unified accounts and stablecoin settlements. This includes not only cryptocurrencies but also stocks, forex, commodities, and even on-chain assets. A similar direction has also appeared in Coinbase's statements; its CEO has mentioned wanting to build an "exchange that trades everything." However, Coinbase emphasizes "on-chain" integration, while Bitget emphasizes "integration," meaning different assets and different on-chain and off-chain transaction models coexist within the same system. Even with the same direction, the pace and path are clearly different. One approach is a more conservative and stable one, such as Binance and OKX. Their overall strategy is to gradually expand TradFi capabilities within the existing crypto trading system. Besides integrating some Ondo tokenized assets into their wallets, they primarily create TradFi assets in a form similar to crypto perpetual contracts, settled in USDT, with no expiration date, emphasizing a more unified in-exchange experience, and being relatively restrained in the number of assets covered. Essentially, they are integrating TradFi into the existing crypto trading paradigm, rather than designing a separate system for it. The other approach is closer to a "structural restructuring" path. Taking Bitget as an example, its actions lean more towards restructuring the entire trading system within the UEX framework: last year, it first integrated the on-chain and CEX account systems; then it introduced RWA assets, bridging the gap between on-chain and traditional assets; and at the beginning of this year, it completed the addition of TradFi asset token perpetual contracts and CFD (Contracts for Difference) and other multi-asset trading tools. Here's a point that many may be unfamiliar with—CFD (Contracts for Difference). This is a TradFi asset introduction strategy that differs from the more conservative approaches of Binance and OKX. CFD is essentially a mature traditional financial trading framework: users do not hold the underlying asset itself, but rather go long or short based on price fluctuations, with profits and losses determined by the bid-ask spread. This system is mainly used in the forex, precious metals, stock index, and commodity markets, and its core features are clear rules, a well-defined cost structure, and a complete margin and risk control mechanism. Essentially, this approach doesn't transform TradFi into Crypto, but rather represents a coexistence of multiple paradigms. Bitget's approach is also more proactive in asset coverage; for example, the platform currently offers over 250 types of equity assets, providing the deepest coverage. Bitget also disclosed that TradFi accounted for over 10% of its total trading volume in January, and this proportion is expected to continue to expand, potentially accelerating the fading out of niche crypto exchanges like crypto CEXs. 2. Intensive On-Chain Deployment of Traditional TradFi Exchanges Traditional TradFi exchanges are also heading in the same direction. Despite the current downturn in crypto market sentiment, never before have TradFi institutions, organizations, and enterprises been as enthusiastic about the crypto market as they are now. Looking at the past three months of 2026, the contrarian bets of traditional TradFi institutions on crypto have been astonishing. ICE invested in OKX at a valuation of $25 billion, a real financial injection; the NYSE has developed tokenization technology and plans to launch a blockchain-based 24/7 trading platform for tokenized stocks and ETFs; Nasdaq received SEC approval to pilot tokenized securities trading, allowing stocks to be traded on-chain and sharing order books with existing systems; Robinhood launched over 2,000 US stock tokens in Europe and plans to launch 24/7 trading and DeFi features in the future. These actions all point to the same thing: traditional exchanges are moving their core assets, such as stocks and ETFs, onto the blockchain and integrating many crypto assets and tools, attempting to gain the biggest advantages of crypto: 24/7 availability, borderlessness, and programmability. From this perspective, crypto CEXs and traditional exchanges are actually forming a consensus: UEX is the future form of trading exchanges. While many are tired of institutional investment, this round is different in that infrastructure and compliance are maturing simultaneously. The fact that 50,000 people chose on-chain trading during this oil price surge demonstrates that the infrastructure has the conditions to attract new users. On the regulatory front, the US SEC's guidance on January 28th categorizes tokenized securities into direct issuance and third-party models, reducing compliance uncertainty, and Congress is also pushing forward with the stablecoin "CLARITY Act." In February of this year, a new policy on RWA tokenization issued by eight Chinese ministries opened a compliance channel for RWA in Hong Kong.

Conclusion

As TradFi continues to be introduced, the boundaries between crypto exchanges and traditional exchanges are rapidly disappearing. But which will ultimately define the next generation of exchanges?

Currently, each has its advantages. Traditional exchanges control the source of assets, compliance systems, and pricing power; crypto exchanges control global distribution, 24/7 trading capabilities, and more flexible account and product structures.

The two are not simply competing, but rather converging in the same direction, becoming a "unified multi-asset trading portal."

However, the current evolution of exchanges surrounding UEX is still in its first stage. Most of it simply involves moving CEX and DEX, as well as Crypto and TradFi assets, onto the same platform. There are still many underlying issues, such as how to ensure unified pricing, risk control, and usage of different assets under the same account. Therefore, the real dividing line is not at the product level, but rather at the fundamental level, such as account systems and capital efficiency. Whoever can first unlock the core capabilities of cross-asset margin and risk models may be closer to the prototype of the next generation of exchanges.

data.title: The severe imbalance between computing power and hardware: Can the US mining bill reshape the crypto landscape?
data.description: Currently, the US focus on the crypto industry is deepening from the crypto market structure to the broader level of infrastructure sovereignty, namely the crypto mining sector. Not long ago, an aide to US Senator Thom Tillis revealed that the draft of the Crypto Market Structure Act, the "CLARITY Act," would be released this week, primarily containing provisions regarding stablecoin yields and rewards. In response, US Senator Cynthia Lummis stated that the latest revisions to the "CLARITY Act" would provide "the strongest protection ever" for DeFi innovators and developers. For a long time, Cynthia Lummis has been actively promoting several cryptocurrency bills, including those concerning crypto market structure and cryptocurrency tax reform, and is committed to institutionalizing Trump's executive order on a strategic Bitcoin reserve. Today, she and U.S. Senator Bill Cassidy jointly introduced the "Mined in America Act," which has received support from the Satoshi Nakamoto Foundation. As a natural extension of Lummis's policy advocacy, this bill aims to comprehensively promote the development of the U.S. cryptocurrency mining industry and formally enshrine the executive order establishing a strategic Bitcoin reserve into the legal system. In its press release, Lummis emphasized that "the U.S. Mining Act will bring the cryptocurrency mining industry back to the United States through forward-looking initiatives to secure the nation's financial future." The U.S. Mining Act: A Dual Strategy of "Industry and Finance" The introduction of the U.S. Mining Act is not merely a simple adjustment to industrial policy, but rather a concentrated manifestation of the U.S.'s dual strategy of "industry and finance" in the cryptocurrency field. From an industry perspective, Dennis Porter, CEO and co-founder of the Satoshi Action Fund, pointed out the core pain point of the current U.S. cryptocurrency mining industry: its heavy reliance on the hardware supply chain. Taking Bitcoin as an example, Hashrate Index data shows that the United States currently controls approximately 37.52% of Bitcoin's hashrate, ranking first globally; Russia and China rank second and third with 16.42% and 11.73% respectively. It's worth noting that the US's increasing market share in Bitcoin hashrate stems from the Chinese government's complete ban on Bitcoin mining in the summer of 2021. This policy shift triggered a global mining "escape," with a large number of mining machines, technical teams, and capital rapidly shifting to regions with cheap electricity and less stringent regulations. The United States, with its mature power grid infrastructure, relatively stable legal environment, and renewable energy resources, became the biggest beneficiary. At that time, its hashrate market share was less than 17%. However, despite the US's transformation from a key player to a global leader, it still lags significantly behind in basic hardware and supply chains. As Dennis Porter stated, "The US controls 38% of the world's Bitcoin hashrate, but 97% of the hardware supporting that hashrate comes from China. This isn't leadership, it's a burden." This market structure of "hashrate in hand, hardware outsourced" undoubtedly poses a potential risk to the development of the US cryptocurrency mining industry. This risk is becoming increasingly apparent against the backdrop of escalating global geopolitical competition. If the supply chain is disrupted, the US cryptocurrency mining industry will face a shrinking crisis, thereby affecting its influence within the cryptocurrency sector. In terms of regulation, in March 2023, the U.S. House of Representatives passed a non-binding resolution, H.Res. 238, which for the first time formally recognized at the federal level the positive value of Proof-of-Work (PoW) Bitcoin mining for national energy efficiency, renewable energy utilization, technological innovation, and job creation. Although this resolution has no legal force, it was the first time Congress officially affirmed the economic and technological value of the mining industry, signifying a shift in its attitude from "wait-and-see" to "acceptance," sending a clear policy signal of friendliness to the market. However, apart from this, there are very few other laws related to cryptocurrency mining in the United States. Therefore, the proposal of the "American Mining Act" is of great significance. It aims to build a virtuous cycle through a certification system and policy support, breaking the U.S.'s dependence on the supply chain and promoting the return of mining hardware manufacturing to the United States. Specifically, the core content of the bill includes four points: 1. Creating a voluntary "U.S. mining" certification system: requiring the U.S. Department of Commerce to establish a voluntary certification program for cryptocurrency mining facilities and pools, and to regulate mining pools and farms; 2. Phased out mining hardware associated with "foreign adversaries": certified mining farms must gradually phase out mining equipment produced by companies associated with "foreign adversaries"; 3. Utilizing existing federal energy and rural programs to support the transition, incorporating certified projects into existing federal programs, rather than creating new spending authorizations; 4. Supporting domestic mining hardware manufacturing: directing the National Institute of Standards and Technology and the manufacturing sector to expand cooperative projects to assist U.S. manufacturers in developing safe and energy-efficient cryptocurrency mining equipment. As can be seen, this bill attempts to reshape the US mining industry chain from its source, localizing core aspects such as equipment manufacturing and mining farm operation, and building a complete closed loop from hardware R&D to computing power output. This can reduce supply chain risks, drive related technology R&D and manufacturing employment, and form a new economic growth point. Cassidy emphasized, "Digital asset mining is an important part of the US economic system and this business should be carried out in the United States. This bill will ensure supply chain security and support US manufacturing." From a financial perspective, the core content of the bill is to rebuild the cryptocurrency mining industry, but its ultimate goal is to achieve a national-level Bitcoin strategic reserve in the United States. As the bill mentions, it formalizes Trump's Bitcoin strategic reserve plan and establishes a Bitcoin strategic reserve project within the Treasury Department. On March 6th of last year, Trump officially signed an executive order authorizing the Treasury Department to establish a "strategic Bitcoin reserve," incorporating approximately 200,000 Bitcoins seized by the Department of Justice into national assets. Back in July 2024, Lummis, a long-time supporter of crypto policy, proposed the "BITCOIN Act of 2024," suggesting the establishment of a federal-level national strategic reserve of 1 million Bitcoins over five years, planned to be gradually implemented through annual purchases. The "US Mining Act" can be seen as a stage in the evolution of this proposal's policy. Its commitment to institutionalizing Bitcoin reserves is both an upgrade of Trump's executive order, granting it long-term stability and legislative legitimacy to avoid the risk of policy reversals due to future government changes, and an affirmation that Bitcoin reserves will become a pillar of the national digital infrastructure strategy, further strengthening the US's policy commitment to becoming a global digital asset center. Similar to the CLARITY Act, the introduction of the US Mining Act is another key milestone in the US digital sovereignty strategy. For the first time, it tightly binds crypto mining companies, hardware supply chains, and national strategic reserves together, aiming to break the structural dependence of "having computing power but relying on foreign hardware," and driving the US to shift from passively accepting the global flow of mining machines to actively building its own industrial ecosystem. Meanwhile, this bill is not an isolated policy measure, but rather a systemic continuation of Trump's executive orders, aiming to elevate temporary executive authorization to a long-term national will. Its significance lies not in its passage, but in declaring to the global market that the United States is defining the future direction of cryptocurrencies through legislative language.
